title: Eid Ceasefire Expires; Cross-Border Shelling Resumes Within Minutes
detail: The five-day Eid al-Fitr ceasefire between Pakistan and Afghanistan expired at midnight on March 24. Within minutes of expiry, Pakistani artillery fired dozens of shells into Afghanistan's Kunar province, striking the Narai and Sarkano districts. Afghan Taliban officials said the renewed shelling killed at least two civilians and wounded eight others. Afghan border forces returned fire, claiming they destroyed three Pakistani military posts and killed one person — claims Pakistan's military did not immediately acknowledge. A local Pakistani official in the northwest accused Afghan forces of initiating the exchange, with each side blaming the other for the breakdown. Pakistan's Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar said the ceasefire 'remains intact' on Pakistan's side as long as the other side does not resume hostilities, a position Afghan officials rejected as contradictory to the artillery fire.

media.caption: Cross-border shelling resumes within minutes of Eid ceasefire expiry

title: TTP Announces End of Eid Ceasefire, Resumes Attacks Inside Pakistan
detail: The Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) announced it had resumed attacks inside Pakistan following the conclusion of its three-day Eid ceasefire. The group, which is separate from but closely aligned with the Afghan Taliban, declared the resumption of its militant campaign in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province and the tribal belt. Pakistani security officials confirmed a series of overnight incidents attributed to TTP fighters. The resumption of TTP operations coincided with the broader ceasefire breakdown between the Afghan Taliban government and Pakistan, underscoring the intertwined nature of the conflict and Pakistan's inability to separate state-to-state fighting from non-state militant activity along the Afghan border.
